title: PMBOK Guide 8th Edition - All 40 Processes Explained

sections.title: Initiate the Project or Phase
sections.intro: This process officially authorizes the start of a project or phase by validating the business case and issuing the project charter.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Business case — establishes the reason and value behind the project and whether the benefit justifies the cost
Benefits management plan — describes how benefits will be delivered and measured
Existing agreements — any contracts or agreements already in place relevant to the project

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment — leveraging expertise to evaluate the business case and project scope
Data gathering — brainstorming, focus groups, and surveys to understand project needs
Conflict management — facilitating differing stakeholder ideas about what the project should deliver
Meetings and facilitation — bringing ideas together through structured discussion
Responsibility assignment matrix (RACI) — defining what roles and responsibilities are needed to do the project work

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Project charter — formally authorizes the project and grants permission to use funding and resources; approved by the project sponsor
Assumption log — documents assumptions made during project initiation

sections.title: Identify Stakeholders
sections.intro: This process identifies all individuals and groups who are or believe themselves to be involved in or impacted by the project, so they can be engaged appropriately.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter — may already list potential stakeholders
Business documents (business case, benefits management plan) — contain stakeholders who need to be engaged
Communications management plan and stakeholder engagement plan — provide existing stakeholder information
Change requests and issue logs — individuals frequently raising changes or issues are stakeholders to engage
Existing agreements — contracts and agreements may identify additional stakeholders

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment — organizational insiders who know which stakeholders need to be involved
Data gathering — questionnaires, surveys, and brainstorming to surface stakeholder information
Data analysis — analyzing stakeholders to understand how to engage each one appropriately
Data representation (stakeholder mapping) — mapping stakeholders by impact and influence to prioritize engagement

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Stakeholder register — the primary list of all identified stakeholders and relevant information about them
Change requests — may be raised for updates to the stakeholder engagement plan
Project management plan updates — communications management plan and risk register may be updated with new stakeholder information
Project document updates — assumption log, issue log, and risk register updated based on stakeholder findings

sections.title: Integrate and Align Project Plan
sections.intro: This process creates the project management plan by integrating all subsidiary plans into one documented baseline that governs future change requests.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter (provides high-level delivery ideas)
Outputs from all other planning processes (all subsidiary plans feed in)
Organizational process assets such as existing templates

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment
Data gathering
Interpersonal and team skills
Meetings
Project canvas

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Project management plan (the integrated, documented plan shared with key stakeholders)

sections.title: Plan Sourcing Strategy
sections.intro: This process creates a clear framework for defining what needs to be acquired externally or internally, how it will be acquired, and when, replacing the former Procurements process.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter (high-level ideas on external sourcing needs)
Project management plan (scope baseline, schedule, quality)
Project documents: milestones, quality metrics, stakeholder register, risk register, project team assignments

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment
Market research (identifying available vendors)
Source selection criteria and analysis
Make-or-buy analysis
Document analysis

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Sourcing strategy plan
Insourcing or outsourcing decisions
Source selection criteria (agreed criteria for choosing vendors, e.g., cost, reputation, speed)

sections.title: Plan Stakeholder Engagement
sections.intro: This process defines how the project team will engage stakeholders effectively throughout the project lifecycle.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter
Project management plan (resources, communications, risk sections)
Project documents: change log, issue log, assumptions log, prior agreements

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment
Benchmarking against past successful stakeholder engagement
Data analysis (e.g., root cause analysis for issues)
Stakeholder prioritization
Data representation: mind mapping, stakeholder engagement assessment matrix

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Stakeholder engagement plan (updated continuously throughout the project)

sections.title: Plan Communications Management
sections.intro: This process establishes how the project will communicate to stakeholders, defining the right channels, frequency, and methods for each audience.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter (stakeholders and prior agreements)
Project documents: stakeholder register
Resource management plan and stakeholder engagement plan

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment
Communication requirements analysis (asking stakeholders how they want to be communicated with)
Communications technology (same room vs. video conferencing tools)
Communication models and methods
Interpersonal and team skills
Data representation using stakeholder engagement assessment matrix

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Communications management plan
Updates to stakeholder engagement plan
Updates to stakeholder register and project schedule (if communications activities are scheduled)

sections.title: Plan Scope Management
sections.intro: This process defines how scope will be gathered, managed, and controlled on the project, producing a roadmap for scope and requirements activities.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter (high-level scope ideas)
Project management plan
Project documents: risks, stakeholder information, existing requirements documentation

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment
Interviews, focus groups, questionnaires, and surveys
Test and inspection planning (to ensure quality activities are planned)

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Scope management plan
Requirements management plan (also known as the business analysis plan, describing how requirements are gathered and elicited)

sections.title: Elicit and Analyze Requirements
sections.intro: This process actively gathers and analyzes project requirements from stakeholders, turning them into documented scope that drives project activities and delivery.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter and business case (high-level requirements ideas)
Prior agreements
Project documents: assumptions log, lessons learned register, stakeholder register
Project management plan: scope management plan and requirements management plan

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment and decision-making techniques
Data gathering (interviews, surveys, workshops)
Data analysis and data representation
Interpersonal and team skills
Nominal group technique (anonymous voting to avoid group bias)
Design thinking, prioritization, and ranking of requirements

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Requirements documentation (may include product backlog, detailed requirements document, prototypes, mockups, acceptance criteria — varies by project approach)

sections.title: Define Scope
sections.intro: This process clearly specifies what is in scope and what is out of scope, and on predictive projects decomposes scope into a work breakdown structure, while on agile projects it produces a product backlog of user stories.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project charter
Assumption log
Project management plan
Requirements documentation

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment (customers and senior users)
Decision-making on what to deliver
Data analysis and scope decomposition into smaller pieces
Facilitation of brainstorming meetings

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Project scope statement (high-level description of scope including key features and deliverables)
Updates to requirements documentation

sections.title: Develop Scope Structure
sections.intro: This process breaks down the defined scope into a work breakdown structure (or agile backlog) at a level where team members can actually execute the work.

subsections.title: Inputs
points: Project management plan
Project documents
Approved changes

subsections.title: Tools & Techniques
points: Expert judgment (customers, senior users, business analysts)
Brainstorming with the project team
Decomposition (breaking work into smaller work packages or user stories)

subsections.title: Outputs
points: Scope baseline
Work breakdown structure (WBS)
WBS dictionary (adds details such as resources, cost, and duration per work package)
User stories (for agile projects)
Product backlog (for agile projects)

sections.title: Plan Resource Management
sections.intro: Defines the process for identifying, acquiring, and managing the people and materials needed to complete the project.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Risk register and stakeholder register to identify who is already involved and may become project resources.
Existing project documents providing context on what needs to be delivered.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Data gathering and expert judgment, including SWOT analysis to find the right resources.
Hierarchical charts such as organizational breakdown structures and organizational charts.
Responsibility assignment matrix (RACI) to assign responsibility, accountability, consultation, and information roles per deliverable.
Text-based lists as a simpler alternative to charts.
Organizational theory to support team engagement and green human resource management (environmental impact of resource use).

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Resource management plan — the process for how resources will be managed on the project.
Team charter — a team agreement on how the group will work together.
Updates to assumptions log and risk register.

sections.title: Estimate Resources
sections.intro: Determines how many people or how much material is needed to complete each project activity.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Resource management plan — the process just created guides the estimation approach.
Schedule and scope baseline — what must be delivered and when affects resource quantity needed.
Project documents including activity lists and cost estimates to assess whether funding exists for planned resources.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Bottom-up, analogous, and parametric estimating techniques.
Project management information system (PMIS) for storing and accessing project data.
Meetings and data gathering with the team.

Artificial intelligence including predictive analytics, virtual reality, augmented reality, branch-and-bound (decision-tree trimming), constructive cost models, and genetic algorithms to identify the optimal resource path.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Resource requirements — the quantity and type of resources needed per activity.
Basis of estimates — documentation of the methodology and assumptions behind each estimate.
Resource breakdown structure — a hierarchy of project team members or materials to be used.

sections.title: Plan Schedule Management
sections.intro: Establishes the policies and procedures for planning, developing, managing, and controlling the project schedule.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project charter — provides high-level milestones as a guide.
Project management plan including scope management plan (what is being delivered) and development approach (waterfall vs. agile).

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from people with scheduling expertise.
Alternatives analysis to evaluate different scheduling approaches and identify the critical path.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Schedule management plan — the defined process for creating, managing, and controlling the project schedule.

sections.title: Develop Schedule
sections.intro: Integrates scope, activity sequences, resource assignments, and duration estimates into an approved schedule baseline.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project charter for high-level schedule insights and milestones.
Project management plan including scope management plan and development approach (waterfall delivers all at end; agile uses a product roadmap with incremental releases).
Project documents: activity list, basis of estimates, duration estimates, milestone list, schedule network diagrams, and existing agreements.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Rolling wave planning — near-term work planned in detail, future work planned at a high level.
Decomposition, precedence diagramming method, logical relationships, leads and lags, and dependencies.
Estimation techniques, reserves, and what-if analysis or simulation.
Schedule compression techniques: fast tracking and crashing.
Critical path method to determine the shortest possible project duration.
Critical chain method, resource leveling, and resource optimization.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Schedule baseline — the approved schedule (Gantt or milestone chart) locked in for waterfall; requires a change request to modify.
Schedule data and project calendars showing resource availability.
Change requests, schedule management plan updates, and basis of estimates.

sections.title: Plan Financial Management
sections.intro: Creates the process for how money, budget, and finances will be managed throughout the project.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project charter for initial financial context.
Project management plan including risk management plan and schedule management plan to understand timing and scope impacts on cost.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from financial specialists.
Data analysis to evaluate budget alternatives.
Meetings to consolidate financial planning decisions.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Financial management plan — the documented process for managing project finances.
Funding strategy — defines whether funding is received all at once or in tranches, whether it is internal or external, and whether mechanisms such as share issuance, bonds, or an IPO are required.

sections.title: Estimate Costs
sections.intro: Develops approximations of the monetary resources needed to complete each project activity based on scope, quality, schedule, and resource requirements.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Quality management plan and scope baseline — higher quality requirements increase cost.
Lessons learned register from prior projects.
Project schedule, risk register, and resource requirements.
Make-or-buy decisions and work package estimates from the WBS.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Bottom-up estimating — aggregating work-package-level estimates to an overall total.
Parametric estimating — using a rate such as cost per meter.
Analogous estimating — using a similar past feature or deliverable as a reference.
Expert judgment, multipoint (three- or six-point) estimating, and voting.
PMIS for storing and retrieving cost and financial data.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Cost estimates — the monetary estimate for each activity or work package.
Basis of estimates — rationale, methodology, and assumptions behind each estimate.
Project document updates including risks and assumptions log.

sections.title: Develop Budget
sections.intro: Aggregates estimated costs of all activities and adds reserves to establish an authorized cost baseline and overall project budget.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project management plan: financial management plan (the agreed process), resource management plan, and scope baseline.
Cost estimates and basis of estimates.
Risk register, project schedule, business case, and benefits management plan.
Prior agreements with vendors or systems that will affect the budget.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from relevant business and financial stakeholders.
Cost aggregation (bottom-up estimating) — rolling up work-package costs to an overall total.
Data analysis and reserves analysis — contingency reserves for known risks, management reserves for unknown in-scope risks.
Historical information review, financing options, and funding limit reconciliation to verify actual spending aligns with the plan.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Cost baseline — the approved budget (work packages + contingency reserves) locked in; requires a change request to modify on a predictive project.
Project funding requirements — the total funds needed including management reserve.
Project document updates including risk register and cost estimate revisions.

sections.title: Plan Risk Management
sections.intro: Establish how risk management activities will be conducted throughout the project by creating a risk management plan.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from a dedicated risk person to help analyze risks
Interviews to gather risk-related insights
Stakeholder analysis to understand risk ownership and tolerances
Meetings to consolidate inputs and agree on the risk process

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Risk management plan — defines the overall risk management process for the project

sections.title: Identify Risks
sections.intro: Identify and document all potential risks (threats and opportunities) that could impact the project, capturing them in the risk register.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: All project management plans (schedule, finance, quality, resources, risk) — risk can come from any area
Cost estimates — incorrect estimates represent potential future risk
Issue log — current issues may trigger future risks
Lessons learned and stakeholder register — for context and risk ownership
Prior agreements that may introduce obligations or constraints

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from people familiar with the project domain
Data analysis: SWOT analysis and root cause analysis (5 Whys, Ishikawa/fishbone diagrams)
Interpersonal and team skills — facilitated meetings
Artificial intelligence to brainstorm risks and potential responses
Prompt lists — organizational lists of previously encountered risks used for brainstorming

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Risk register — records each risk with its likelihood, impact, and current controls
Risk report — high-level overview of all risks, urgent items, and planned responses sent to stakeholders
Updates to assumptions log, issue log, and lessons learned

sections.title: Perform Risk Analysis
sections.intro: Analyze identified risks to assess their likelihood and potential impact so that the most critical risks can be prioritized for response planning.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project management plan including the risk management plan — follow the agreed process
Scope, schedule, and cost baselines — changes to these are primary risk targets
Project documents (e.g., risk register) and enterprise environmental factors such as templates

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from team members and stakeholders
Interviews and facilitation to gather probability and impact assessments
Risk categorization — grouping risks into high, medium, or low tiers
Data analysis: simulations (e.g., Monte Carlo) to model cost and schedule probability distributions
Decision tree analysis and influence diagrams
Probability and impact matrix — rates each risk on both dimensions to assign an overall rating

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Updated risk register — risks ranked and rated by probability and impact
Risk report — distributed to stakeholders with the analyzed risk information

sections.title: Plan Risk Responses
sections.intro: Develop options and actions to address overall project risk and individual risks, selecting appropriate response strategies to reduce threats and enhance opportunities.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Risk management plan — defines the agreed process and thresholds
Cost baseline and resource management plan
Project schedule, risk register, and risk report — provide the full list of analyzed risks to respond to

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment
Interviews and facilitation
Strategies for threats: accept, mitigate, escalate, transfer, and one additional strategy
Strategies for opportunities — mirror strategies applied to positive risks
Contingent response strategies — actions triggered only if specific conditions occur
Strategies for overall project risk

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Change requests — responding to risks may require changes to baselined items
Updates to project management plans affected by chosen responses
Updates to project documents: assumptions log, cost forecasts, risk register (with response details), and risk report

sections.title: Manage Project Execution
sections.intro: Direct and perform the work defined in the project management plan to produce the project deliverables.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from team members and subject-matter experts
PMIS (Project Management Information System) to gather and track project data
Meetings to coordinate and integrate work across the team

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Deliverables — the actual value or product being produced for the organization
Work performance data showing scope delivery, cost, schedule status, and defect counts
Issue and change logs capturing any problems or change requests encountered
Project management plan updates as execution reveals needed adjustments
Project document updates including activities, assumptions, lessons learned, risks, and stakeholder information

sections.title: Manage Quality Assurance
sections.intro: Apply planned quality processes and audits to ensure the project is using the right practices and continuously improving.

subsections.name: Key Distinction
bullets: Quality assurance = confirming the right processes are being followed (process-focused)
Quality control = confirming deliverables meet defined specifications (product-focused)

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project management plan
Project documents
Organizational policies, procedures, and regulations

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Audits to review whether processes comply with best practices
Checklists for systematic quality verification
Data representation tools: affinity diagrams, flowcharts, Ishikawa (cause-and-effect) diagrams
Decision-making, problem solving, and process improvement techniques

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Quality report summarizing quality findings and issues
Change requests to address defects or quality gaps
Project management plan updates for any quality-driven component changes

sections.title: Manage Project Knowledge
sections.intro: Leverage existing organizational knowledge and create new knowledge to improve project outcomes and build lessons learned.

subsections.name: Key Concepts
bullets: Explicit knowledge can be codified in words and numbers (e.g., documented processes)
Tacit knowledge is experiential — beliefs, insights, and intuition that are harder to document

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project management plans, lessons learned register, stakeholder register
Deliverables themselves as a source of knowledge

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from those with relevant expertise
Knowledge management and information management systems (PMIS)
After-action reviews (AARs) and postmortems — similar to retrospectives
Storytelling, retrospective meetings, interpersonal and team skills, facilitation

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Lessons learned register updated with new insights
Project management plan updates reflecting process improvements

sections.title: Manage Stakeholder Engagement
sections.intro: Work with stakeholders throughout the project to understand their needs, address concerns, and foster appropriate involvement.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Communications management plan — reviewing whether communication is happening in the right ways and at the right times
Risk management plan, stakeholder engagement plan, and change management plan
Change log and issue log — high volumes of changes or issues from one person signal a need for deeper engagement

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment
Communication skills: giving feedback, managing conflict, cultural awareness, negotiation
Observation and conversation (job shadowing) to understand stakeholder context
Political awareness of how the organization operates
Ground rules for meetings to maintain productive interactions

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Change requests arising from stakeholder interactions
Updates to communications management plan or stakeholder engagement plan if gaps are identified
Issue and change log updates

sections.title: Manage Communications
sections.intro: Ensure timely and appropriate creation, collection, distribution, storage, and retrieval of project information for all stakeholders.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Resource management plan — guides communication with project resources
Communications management plan — defines methods and processes for stakeholder communication
Stakeholder engagement plan — identifies who to engage and how
Change log, issue log, quality report, and risk report — used to determine who needs increased communication
Stakeholder register and work performance reports — show project status and who may need attention

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Communications technology (e.g., video conferencing and other platforms)
Communication methods and communication skills
Nonverbal communication awareness (words are only ~7% of overall communication; body language and tone are dominant)
Project reporting, interpersonal and team skills (e.g., active listening)
Meetings to bring everything together

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Project communications — the actual information shared with stakeholders
Updates to communications management plan or stakeholder management plan if process gaps are found
Project document updates including lessons learned, issue log, risk register, and stakeholder register

sections.title: Acquire Resources
sections.intro: Obtain the physical and human resources needed to carry out project work.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Resource management plan and project documents describing resource needs
Organizational process assets such as policies for resource acquisition

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Decision-making to select among resource options
Interpersonal and team skills including negotiation and problem-solving to obtain resources from the business
Pre-assignment when someone has already agreed to work on the project
Virtual teams using video conferencing, online portals, and messaging systems

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Physical or virtual resource assignments confirming what resources have been obtained
Resource calendars showing when each resource is available
Project team assignments documenting who is assigned to which work
Change requests if required resources cannot be obtained, potentially adjusting resources or scope
Updates to project documents, project management plans, and organizational process assets related to resources

sections.title: Lead the Team
sections.intro: Direct and develop the project team to perform project work and achieve project objectives.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Resource management plan defining the process for managing and leading team resources
Project documents including the issue log, schedule, and resource calendars showing availability
Team charter documenting agreed ways of working within the team
Team performance assessments to gauge how team members are performing

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Colocation (putting everyone together) or virtual teams using Slack, messaging, or video conferencing
Communication technology and interpersonal and team skills to manage team dynamics
Problem-solving using the six thinking hats (new in PMBOK 8th edition) and retrospectives to improve processes
Recognition and rewards, individual and team assessments, and meetings
Emotional intelligence, organizational cultural intelligence, leadership, and the Tuckman ladder
Virtual collaboration tools and the PMIS to house and access team information

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Team performance assessments evaluating how the team is performing
Change requests if adjustments to how the team is managed are needed
Updates to the resource management plan, issue log, and team charter as needed

sections.title: Implement Risk Responses
sections.intro: Execute the agreed risk response plans to address threats and opportunities that occur during project execution.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Risk management plan defining the process for handling risks
Risk register and risk report detailing identified risks and their status for stakeholders

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment from people with expertise in the affected area
Influencing to enact responses across the organization
PMIS housing risk information and impacted project components

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Change requests to adjust aspects of the project resulting from risk responses
Updates to the risk register and risk report reflecting response actions taken
Potential updates to team assignments if risk responses affect resource allocation

sections.title: Monitor and Control Project Performance
sections.intro: Collects work performance data and turns it into work performance reports to communicate project status to stakeholders.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment and data analysis
Decision-making
Meetings
Project dashboards to visualize performance data
Visual controls and information radiators displaying status in team areas

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Work performance reports sent to stakeholders showing project progress
Change requests arising from performance issues
Project management plan updates to any component
Updates to issues, risks, and schedule forecasts

sections.title: Assess and Implement Changes
sections.intro: Evaluates submitted change requests through a formal change control process and implements those that are approved.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Change management plan describing how changes are managed
Configuration management plan for version control and baselining
Scope, schedule, and cost baselines that may be impacted
Change log, basis of estimates, and requirements traceability matrix
Risk report and work performance reports
Change requests themselves

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment to assess impact and alternatives
Change control tools and data analysis to explore options
Decision-making including voting on the best path forward
Meetings and integrated change control
Managing the product backlog (agile: product owner reprioritizes without formal change control)

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Approved change requests endorsed by the change control board
Project management plan updates for components affected by the change

sections.title: Monitor Stakeholder Engagement
sections.intro: Oversees stakeholder relationships throughout the project to ensure information flows smoothly in both directions.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Communications management plan for stakeholder communication processes
Stakeholder engagement plan
Change management plan identifying who to consult on changes
Risk management plan for risk owners and emerging risks
Change logs and issue logs

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment
Communication skills
Conflict negotiation and cultural awareness
Interpersonal skills to smooth stakeholder issues
Ground rules for team meetings

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Change requests resulting from stakeholder engagement issues
Updates to communications management plan or stakeholder engagement plan
Updates to change log, issue log, lessons learned, and stakeholder register

sections.title: Monitor Communications
sections.intro: Reviews actual communications sent and received to confirm they are reaching the right people at the right time and producing the desired results.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Resource management plan, communications management plan, and stakeholder engagement plan
Project documents including issues and lessons learned
Work performance data representing actual communications sent and results obtained

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment to assess communication effectiveness
PMIS (e.g., Jira, Confluence, SharePoint) for storing project information
Data representation using a stakeholder engagement matrix
Observation, conversation, and meetings

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Work performance information synthesizing communication data into meaningful conclusions
Work performance reports shared with stakeholders as needed
Change requests
Updates to communications or stakeholder engagement plans
Updates to issues log and stakeholder register

sections.title: Monitor and Control Scope
sections.intro: Tracks project scope against the baseline to prevent scope creep and gold-plating while ensuring deliverables meet quality standards.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Scope management plan and quality management plan (including test/evaluation approach)
Performance measurement baseline
Quality test and evaluation documents, quality metrics
Approved change requests and deliverables

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Data analysis including defect analysis and performance reviews
Audits and inspections
Data representations
Process automations
Testing and product evaluations

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Quality report summarizing defect rates and whether deliverables are ready for release
Verified deliverables (quality-tested items ready for sponsor acceptance)
Change requests for defects or issues found
Updates to quality control measures, scope and quality management plans, and requirements documentation

sections.title: Validate Scope
sections.intro: Obtains formal acceptance of completed deliverables from the project sponsor or customer before releasing them.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Scope baseline
Quality reports indicating defect levels and readiness
Verified deliverables (already quality-tested)

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Data analysis and inspection of deliverables
Decision-making
Customer talks and tests to discuss issues and workarounds
Process analysis and review meetings

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Accepted deliverables formally approved by the customer or project sponsor
Change requests for unaccepted items
Quality reports and work performance information

sections.title: Monitor and Control Resourcing
sections.intro: Track resource usage and performance, identifying and resolving constraints to keep the team and physical resources on track.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Problem-solving methods such as value stream mapping to identify bottlenecks.
Theory of constraints — used when work piles up in a specific area (e.g., too many items in testing).
Control charts to monitor resource performance trends.
Branch and bound for optimization decisions.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Work performance information reflecting current resource status.
Change requests to address resource issues.
Project management plan updates — resource management plan, schedule baseline, cost baseline.
Project document updates — issues log, lessons learned register, physical resource assignments, and resource breakdown structure (team structure changes).

sections.title: Monitor and Control Schedule
sections.intro: Ensure the project schedule remains on track by comparing actual progress to the baseline and applying corrective actions as needed.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Schedule management plan outlining how schedule will be controlled.
Scope baseline and performance measurement baseline (e.g., planned delivery date vs. actual).
Product backlog, project documents, and work performance data.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Earned value analysis, burn-up/burndown charts, and trend analysis.
Critical path method — may be updated as the project progresses.
Resource optimization to shuffle people and realign capacity.
Project Management Information System (PMIS) to consolidate data.
Schedule compression — fast-tracking or crashing.
Agile techniques: velocity, daily stand-ups, sprint reviews, backlog refinement.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Work performance information comparing schedule progress and scope delivered.
Schedule forecasts projecting future performance.
Change requests for schedule baseline adjustments.
Updates to project management plan and project documents (lessons learned, project schedule).

sections.title: Monitor and Control Finance
sections.intro: Keep the project budget under control by comparing actual costs against the cost baseline and using earned value analysis to forecast financial health.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Financial management plan and cost baseline defining the approved budget.
Performance measurement baseline used to assess cost variance.
Lessons learned register and project funding requirements (incremental or lump-sum funding).

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment for financial assessment.
Data analysis including earned value analysis — compares value delivered to value that should have been delivered.
To-complete performance index (TCPI) to determine cost efficiency needed to meet objectives.
Project Management Information System (PMIS).

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Work performance information showing whether the budget is on track.
Cost forecasts for remaining work.
Change requests if cost baseline or financial management plan needs updating.
Project document updates — issues, assumptions, and basis-of-estimates changes.

sections.title: Monitor Risks
sections.intro: Continuously track identified risks, identify and analyze new risks, and plan updated responses throughout the project lifecycle.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Risk management plan describing the monitoring process.
Issues log, risk register, and risk report (highlighting top risks and responses for stakeholders).
Work performance data and work performance reports that may affect risk monitoring.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Technical performance analysis to assess whether technical objectives are being met.
Reserve analysis — contingency reserve for identified risks; management reserve for unforeseen in-scope work.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Work performance information on risk status.
Change requests to implement risk responses that affect scope, schedule, or cost.
Updates to project management plan components (schedule, scope, cost) in response to risks.
Project document updates — assumptions log, issues log, risk register, and risk report.

sections.title: Close the Project or Phase
sections.intro: Formally confirm acceptance of all deliverables, finalize vendor claims, capture final lessons learned, transfer outputs to operations, and archive project information.

subsections.name: Key Activities
bullets: Confirm delivery and formal acceptance of deliverables — quality-verified by the team, validated and accepted by the project sponsor.
Finalize any open procurement claims with vendors.
Measure product benefits against the business case and benefits management plan.
Conduct a final lessons learned session.
Transfer all deliverables and operational responsibilities to the business.
Close financial accounts and archive all project information.

subsections.name: Inputs
bullets: Project management plan — all components (scope, schedule, cost, resources, risks, communications, stakeholders).
Project documents — assumptions, change log, issues log, lessons learned, milestones.
Quality reports, risk register, accepted deliverables, business case, benefits management plan, and agreements.

subsections.name: Tools & Techniques
bullets: Expert judgment for final evaluation.
Data analysis — trend and variance analysis across the project for lessons learned.
Meetings to consolidate final information.

subsections.name: Outputs
bullets: Final product, service, or result transitioned to the business/operations.
Final report summarizing project performance, variances, and lessons learned.
Updates to organizational process assets — lessons learned repository and revised templates.
